creator: wollman@csail.mit.edu
text:
<<On Thu, 12 Mar 2009 20:39:37 +0000, David Wood <david@wood2.org.uk> said:

> This PR offers a possible enhancement: making rlm_perl and rlm_python 
> optional. FreeRADIUS 2 does have unlang, but my understanding is that 
> rlm_perl and rlm_python are regarded as core functionality for 
> FreeRADIUS 2.

What exactly do you mean by that?  It's not something I'm using, it's
not something that has to be there in order for the service to work
properly, and given that it is a security-related service I'd rather
reduce the bloat as much as possible.  (Nor do I want to have to
rebuild the FreeRADIUS port every time some scripting language I don't
use has another security hole in a little-used bundled library.)

> I realise that embedded users may want the total space taken when 
> installing FreeRADIUS to be little as possible, which is why I left the 
> NOPERL knob from net/freeradius intact. That tears out the dependency on 
> Perl, but does not attempt to deal with the behaviour of configure.in. 
> If you want to use this, build the port with make -DWITH_NOPERL.

Non-"options" port knobs are exceedingly unfriendly.
/var/db/ports/freeradius2/options is a much better place to record how
a port was built than a sticky note on my desktop, and more likely to
be understood by someone else should I get hit by a bus walking to
lunch tomorrow.

> There seems little point having an option not to build rlm_perl whilst 
> still having Perl on the system - it will save very little space. 
> rlm_perl can be tricky to build, but I've not had any reports of it 
> failing to build successfully via the port.

Well, ideally one would like to differentiate between build-time
dependencies and run-time dependencies, which "USE_PERL=yes" doesn't
do.

> I have not yet received a request for making rlm_python - and hence the 
> dependency on Python - optional. Is this something that the submitter or 
> someone he knows would use?

It's something that I am using in production right now.  FreeRADIUS is
obviously used in many different environments, with widely varying
requirements; what I need for my wireless network access-control
project is going to be different from what the few remaining dialup
ISPs need.

-GAWollman
